figures.caption: Fig. 15: Qualitative comparison of ablation studies. Omitting any component results in reduced rendering and tracking accuracy.
Fig. 14: Qualitative comparison of novel view synthesis on Neural 3D Video dataset [7]. PaMoSplat demonstrates exceptional rendering fidelity, preserving fine details effectively.

TABLE I: Per-scene quantitative comparisons of novel view synthesis on PanopticSports dataset [29]. The results are based on official code implementations of each corresponding method. Our results achieves the highest metrics (PSNR ↑, SSIM ↑, LPIPS ↓).
Fig. 12: More optical flow visualization. PamoSplat even exceeds the performance of the front-end optical flow predictor RAFT.
TABLE VI: Per-scene quantitative comparisons of novel view synthesis in nighttime scenes on Neural 3D Video dataset [7]. Our results achieve the highest metric PSNR.
Fig. 13: More Gaussian part visualizations. For the self-captured dataset, we also provide Gaussian field color visualizations to facilitate cross-referencing.
Fig. 11: Qualitative comparisons of calculated flow. PaMoSplat produces less noise and reveals sharper boundaries.

Fig. 2: Overview of the PaMoSplat pipeline. PaMoSplat introduces Gaussian parts in the initial timestamp, which are shifted in subsequent timestamps based on optical flow. Additionally, PaMoSplat incorporates some rigid constraints and flow-supervised losses to further refine the dynamic scene representation.

Fig. 20: Rendering results in daytime scene on Neural 3D Video dataset [7]. While PaMoSplat is affected by the results of the 3DGS pipeline in initial timestamp, leading to noticeable distortions in the far-field from novel viewpoints, it still preserves higher rendering quality in the dynamic near-field.

Fig. 8: Qualitative comparisons of novel view synthesis. We zoom in on the main comparison baselines to highlight details. With prior motion and optical flow supervision, PaMoSplat demonstrates a significant advantage in modeling highly dynamic elements, even with the sparse view (5 training cameras in the self-captured dataset displayed in the lower right corner).

Fig. 16: Experiments on parameter influence. Reducing the number of training cameras or introducing noise into camera parameters leads to degradation in rendering and tracking quality. While part segmentation granularity shows some robustness, performance deteriorates significantly under severe under-segmentation.
Fig. 17: Model-agnostic study. PamoSplat exhibits consistently stable performance across different models.

Fig. 10: Qualitative comparisons of 3D trajectory (projected to 2D). Our results (green) align more closely with the ground truth (red) values compared to D-3DGS [29] (blue). Where ground truth is noisy, our results potentially offer greater accuracy.
